title: Listening - Part 1
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear three different extracts. For questions 1 – 6, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear. There are two questions for each extract.

extracts.context: You hear two colleagues, a manager and a senior executive, discussing a recent corporate restructuring.
extracts.audio_script: Man: Well, Sonia, the dust has finally settled on the departmental merger. I must say, [1]the lack of transparency during the transition was quite disconcerting for my team[/1]. ||Woman: I take your point, Ryan, but the board felt a rapid rollout was essential to prevent productivity slumping. ||Man: Perhaps, but it’s thrown up issues with role overlap that we’re still unpicking. ||Woman: That’s to be expected. However, [2]what really strikes me is how the junior staff have taken the initiative despite the ambiguity[/2]. They've really stepped up. ||Man: I agree, their resilience was the silver lining. But moving forward, we can't just rely on their goodwill. We need a concrete hierarchy. ||Woman: Absolutely. We’re drafting a revised organizational chart as we speak to clarify those blurred lines.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Well, Sonia, the dust has finally settled on the departmental merger. I must say, the lack of transparency during the transition was quite disconcerting for my team.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I take your point, Ryan, but the board felt a rapid rollout was essential to prevent productivity slumping.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Perhaps, but it’s thrown up issues with role overlap that we’re still unpicking.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: That’s to be expected. However, what really strikes me is how the junior staff have taken the initiative despite the ambiguity. They've really stepped up.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I agree, their resilience was the silver lining. But moving forward, we can't just rely on their goodwill. We need a concrete hierarchy.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Absolutely. We’re drafting a revised organizational chart as we speak to clarify those blurred lines.

questions.question: What is the man's main complaint about the restructuring process?

options.A: The speed at which the changes were implemented.
options.B: The way information was withheld from the staff.
options.C: The resulting confusion regarding job descriptions.

questions.explanation: The man mentions that the 'lack of transparency' (evidence [1]) was disconcerting, referring to the poor communication during the shift.

questions.question: What has the woman been particularly impressed by?

options.A: The board's decisive leadership during the crisis.
options.B: The speed with which new hierarchies were established.
options.C: The proactive attitude shown by less experienced employees.

questions.explanation: She notes that 'junior staff have taken the initiative' (evidence [2]), showing she was impressed by their proactivity.

extracts.context: You hear two friends talking about a documentary they both watched regarding urban farming.
extracts.audio_script: Woman: So, did you catch that documentary on vertical farming last night? Man: I did. [1]I found the presenter’s cynical tone regarding traditional agriculture a bit hard to swallow, to be honest[/1]. Woman: Really? I thought she was just being realistic about the environmental toll. Though, I must admit, [2]the segment on the energy costs of LED lighting made me question if it's actually a sustainable alternative[/2] at all. Man: Exactly! It’s all well and good growing lettuce in a basement, but the carbon footprint of the electricity used is astronomical. Woman: Still, the tech is fascinating. If they could power it all through renewables, it would be a game changer for inner cities. Man: Possibly, but I’m still not convinced it'll ever be more than a niche hobby for the wealthy. Woman: Well, it’s early days. The industry is still finding its feet.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: So, did you catch that documentary on vertical farming last night?

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I did. I found the presenter’s cynical tone regarding traditional agriculture a bit hard to swallow, to be honest.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Really? I thought she was just being realistic about the environmental toll. Though, I must admit, the segment on the energy costs of LED lighting made me question if it's actually a sustainable alternative at all.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Exactly! It’s all well and good growing lettuce in a basement, but the carbon footprint of the electricity used is astronomical.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Still, the tech is fascinating. If they could power it all through renewables, it would be a game changer for inner cities.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Possibly, but I’m still not convinced it'll ever be more than a niche hobby for the wealthy.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Well, it’s early days. The industry is still finding its feet.

questions.question: What did the man dislike about the documentary?

options.A: The biased attitude of the person hosting it.
options.B: The lack of focus on traditional farming methods.
options.C: The unrealistic solutions proposed for urban areas.

questions.explanation: He says the 'presenter’s cynical tone' (evidence [1]) was 'hard to swallow', indicating he found the host biased or unpleasant.

questions.question: What is the woman's current reservation about vertical farming?

options.A: The limited variety of crops that can be grown.
options.B: The high prices of the final products.
options.C: The amount of power required to maintain the systems.

questions.explanation: She mentions that 'energy costs of LED lighting' (evidence [2]) made her doubt its sustainability.

extracts.context: You hear two university students from Ireland discussing their upcoming final year project.
extracts.audio_script: Man: I’m starting to panic a bit about the dissertation topic, Emily. I’ve been plodding through the literature, but nothing’s clicking. Woman: Don't let it get to you, Connor. [1]It’s often a case of just picking a thread and seeing where it leads[/1], rather than waiting for a 'eureka' moment. Man: Easy for you to say! You’ve already got your data sources lined up. I’m worried my focus is too broad. Woman: Well, my supervisor did warn me that [2]I might have been overly ambitious with the number of interviews I’ve planned[/2]. I might have to scale it back if I want to finish by May. Man: Actually, that makes me feel slightly better. I thought I was the only one struggling with the scope. Woman: Far from it. Half the class is in the same boat, honestly. We just need to keep chipping away at it.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I’m starting to panic a bit about the dissertation topic, Emily. I’ve been plodding through the literature, but nothing’s clicking.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Don't let it get to you, Connor. It’s often a case of just picking a thread and seeing where it leads, rather than waiting for a 'eureka' moment.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Easy for you to say! You’ve already got your data sources lined up. I’m worried my focus is too broad.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Well, my supervisor did warn me that I might have been overly ambitious with the number of interviews I’ve planned. I might have to scale it back if I want to finish by May.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Actually, that makes me feel slightly better. I thought I was the only one struggling with the scope.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Far from it. Half the class is in the same boat, honestly. We just need to keep chipping away at it.

questions.question: What advice does the woman give the man regarding his dissertation?

options.A: To wait for a truly original idea to emerge.
options.B: To start writing even if the direction is unclear.
options.C: To narrow down his research focus immediately.

questions.explanation: She suggests 'picking a thread and seeing where it leads' (evidence [1]) instead of waiting for a perfect moment, implying he should just start.

questions.question: What does the woman admit about her own project?

options.A: She is struggling to find enough participants for her research.
options.B: She has underestimated the time needed for data analysis.
options.C: She may have set herself an unrealistic workload.

questions.explanation: She admits she was 'overly ambitious with the number of interviews' (evidence [2]), which means her workload is currently too high.
